topics: Poem: Jalate Chalo (Keep Lighting the Lamps)
subject: Hindi

sections.heading: 🔥 Quest Begins: What is this Poem About?
sections.body: Welcome, young wizard! This poem is written by Dwarka Prasad Maheshwari. It tells us to keep doing good deeds for others, just like lighting a diya (lamp) in the dark. Even if the world feels dark and hopeless, one small act of kindness can bring light. Think of it like Diwali — even one diya can brighten a whole room!
keyPoints: The poem says: keep lighting lamps full of love and care for others
Even when the world feels sad or dark, never give up hope
The poet encourages us to keep doing good work, like a true hero on a quest

sections.heading: 🌑 The Dragon of Darkness: Symbols in the Poem
sections.body: In this poem, 'darkness' or 'timir' is not just the night — it means all the sadness, problems, and hopelessness in the world. The 'diya' or lamp is not just a real lamp — it means our good deeds, love, and hope. Just like in cricket, one good player can turn the whole match around, one good person can change the world! The poem also uses 'Amavasya' to mean the darkest time, and 'Poornima' to mean a bright, happy world.
keyPoints: Darkness (timir/andhera) = problems, sadness, hopelessness in the world
Lamp (diya) = our good deeds, love, and hope for others
Amavasya = the worst, darkest times; Poornima = a bright, peaceful world

sections.heading: ⚔️ The Hero's Power: Keep Going, Never Stop!
sections.body: The poem says that long ago, someone first lit a lamp and accepted the challenge of fighting darkness. That brave act started a great story — just like the first player who scored a century in cricket! The poem tells us that this story of the lamp fighting the storm has been going on for ages, and it will keep going. Every good person who works for others adds to this story. You are part of this quest too!
keyPoints: The first person who did good work accepted a great challenge, like a hero
The fight between light and darkness has been going on since ancient times
Every small good deed you do keeps this story alive — you are a hero!

sections.heading: 🌕 Mastery of the Moon: Amavasya and Poornima
sections.body: The moon does not make its own light — it shines with the Sun's light, just like a mirror! The moon slowly grows bigger each night until Poornima (full moon), when we can see it fully. Then it slowly becomes smaller until Amavasya (new moon), when we cannot see it at all. The days when the moon grows are called Shukla Paksha (bright half), and the days when it shrinks are called Krishna Paksha (dark half). You can check this in your own home calendar!
keyPoints: Poornima = full moon night, the sky is very bright; Amavasya = no moon, the sky is very dark
Shukla Paksha = moon growing bigger day by day; Krishna Paksha = moon getting smaller
The moon has no light of its own — it reflects sunlight, like a cricket ground floodlight reflecting off a white ball!

sections.heading: ✨ The Wizard's Spell: Language Magic in the Poem
sections.body: This poem has a special magic — every line takes almost the same time to read or sing, just like beats in a song or taal in tabla! The poet also uses 'sa/si/se' to show similarity, like 'Amavasya nisha-si' meaning 'a night like Amavasya'. Words like 'jalate chalo' (keep lighting) show that the good work must never stop — it is an ongoing quest, not a one-time action. Changing even one word changes the whole meaning, just like changing one player changes a cricket team!
keyPoints: All lines of the poem take almost equal time to read — this is the poem's special rhythm or laya
'Sa/Si/Se' is used to compare things, like saying 'sweet as jalebi'
'Jalate chalo' means keep doing it always — the quest for goodness never ends!

summary: Chapter 7 'Jalate Chalo' is a heroic poem by Dwarka Prasad Maheshwari that calls us — like brave questers — to keep lighting lamps of love and good deeds even when the world feels dark and hopeless. The lamp is a symbol of hope and kindness, and darkness is a symbol of problems and sadness. The poem tells us that this great battle between light and darkness has always been going on, and every single good deed — no matter how small — helps win this quest. The chapter also teaches us about the science of the moon: how it grows from Amavasya (no moon) to Poornima (full moon) through Shukla Paksha and Krishna Paksha. Finally, we learn about the beautiful rhythm and language of poetry, where words like 'sa/si/se' are used to compare things, and every line flows like music. The message is simple and powerful: never give up, keep spreading love and light, and one day the darkness will definitely be defeated!
